cards.text: To create an alias template: {{c1::template}} <typename T> {{c2::using}} VectorPtr = std::vector<T>*;

cards.back: A template that creates a type alias, allowing you to create convenient shortcuts for complex template types.

cards.question: What does this create?
cards.answer: Creates an int* (pointer to int). Ptr<int> is an alias for int*.
cards.explanation: Alias templates substitute the parameter and create a type alias.

cards.text: Alias templates use the {{c1::using}} keyword with a {{c2::template}} parameter list.

cards.question: What's the benefit of this alias?
cards.answer: Shorter, more readable syntax than repeatedly writing std::vector<T>.
cards.explanation: Alias templates reduce boilerplate in template-heavy code.

cards.front: What's the difference between a type alias and an alias template?
cards.back:
Type alias: Fixed type alias (using IntVec = std::vector<int>)
Alias template: Parameterized (template<typename T> using Vec = std::vector<T>)

cards.question: What does this alias template do?
cards.code:
template <typename T>
using StringMap = std::map<std::string, T>;
cards.answer: Creates a shortcut for maps with string keys and template-parameter values.

cards.text: Unlike {{c1::typedef}}, {{c2::using}} can be used to create alias templates with template parameters.

cards.back: No. Only using can create alias templates. typedef cannot be templated.

cards.explanation:
Use alias templates to:
  • Simplify complex template types
  • Create domain-specific type shortcuts
  • Partially specialize template parameters
  • Improve code readability
Common in template-heavy libraries.
examples: template<typename V> using StrMap = std::map<std::string, V>;

cards.front: Do alias templates create new types?
cards.back: No. They create alternative names for existing types, not new distinct types.

cards.text: Alias templates use {{c1::template}} with {{c2::using}}: template<typename T> using Ptr = T*;
Unlike {{c1::typedef}}, {{c2::using}} declarations can be templated to create alias templates.
Alias templates create {{c1::alternative names}} for types, not {{c2::new distinct types}}.
Alias templates use {{c1::template}} with {{c2::using}}: template<typename T> using Ptr = T*;
Unlike {{c1::typedef}}, {{c2::using}} declarations can be templated to create alias templates.
